meta.description: What interviewers look for: positive and negative signals by interview type and level

dimensions.name: Problem Solving
positive_signals: Clarifies problem before coding — asks about constraints, edge cases, input size
Thinks through multiple approaches before committing
Identifies optimal time/space complexity upfront
Breaks problem into sub-problems naturally
Recognizes patterns (sliding window, two pointers, BFS, etc.)
Handles edge cases proactively (empty input, single element, overflow)
Can adapt when initial approach hits a wall
negative_signals: Jumps straight into coding without understanding the problem
Gets stuck on one approach and can't pivot
Misses obvious edge cases even after prompting
Cannot analyze time/space complexity of own solution
Needs heavy hints to reach even a brute-force solution
Confuses problem with a similar but different problem

dimensions.name: Code Quality
positive_signals: Clean, readable code with meaningful variable names
Modular — extracts helper functions naturally
Consistent style and formatting
Handles errors and edge cases in code, not just verbally
Code runs correctly on first or second attempt
Uses language idioms appropriately
negative_signals: Spaghetti code — deeply nested, long functions
Single-letter variables everywhere
Code doesn't compile or has many syntax errors
Copy-paste duplication instead of abstraction
Ignores error handling entirely
Over-engineers with unnecessary abstractions

dimensions.name: Communication
positive_signals: Thinks aloud — interviewer can follow the reasoning
Explains WHY they chose an approach, not just what
Asks clarifying questions at the right moments
Acknowledges trade-offs between approaches
Responds well to hints — incorporates them smoothly
Tests own code by tracing through examples verbally
negative_signals: Silent for long stretches — interviewer has no idea what they're thinking
Can't explain own code when asked
Ignores interviewer's hints or suggestions
Defensive when approach is questioned
Over-explains trivial things, under-explains complex ones

dimensions.name: Testing & Verification
positive_signals: Tests with normal cases, edge cases, and corner cases
Dry-runs code mentally or on paper before declaring done
Catches own bugs during review
Suggests how they would write unit tests
negative_signals: Says 'I think this works' without testing
Only tests the happy path
Can't identify bugs when pointed to them

level_calibration.E3: Solve 1-2 medium problems cleanly. Brute-force is OK if they can discuss optimal. Communication matters but bar is lower.
level_calibration.E4: Solve mediums quickly, make progress on hards. Must reach optimal for mediums. Clean code expected.
level_calibration.E5: Solve hards. Discuss trade-offs deeply. Code should be production-quality. Drive the conversation.
level_calibration.E6: Solve hards efficiently. Discuss system-level implications. Code quality is exemplary. Mentor-like communication.

dimensions.name: STAR Structure
positive_signals: Clear Situation — sets context concisely (team size, project scope, stakes)
Specific Task — what was THEIR responsibility, not the team's
Detailed Action — concrete steps THEY took, not vague 'we did X'
Quantified Result — measurable impact (%, time saved, users affected)
Keeps answers focused — 2-3 minutes, not 10-minute monologues
negative_signals: Rambling story with no clear structure
Can't distinguish their contribution from the team's
Vague actions: 'I helped coordinate' with no specifics
No measurable result or impact
Story doesn't match the question asked
Answers are too short — no depth or reflection

dimensions.name: Self-Awareness & Growth
positive_signals: Honest about failures — not humble-brags disguised as failures
Shows what they LEARNED and how they CHANGED behavior
Acknowledges their own role in problems, not just external factors
Can discuss strengths without arrogance and weaknesses without deflection
Demonstrates growth trajectory over time
negative_signals: Blames others for every failure
Failure stories that are actually successes in disguise
Cannot name a real weakness or area for growth
Takes zero accountability for anything that went wrong
No evidence of learning from past experiences

dimensions.name: Meta Core Values Alignment
positive_signals: Move Fast: shows bias for action, comfort with imperfect information
Focus on Impact: prioritizes high-leverage work, kills low-value projects
Be Bold: takes calculated risks, proposes ambitious solutions
Be Open: gives and receives direct feedback, transparent about trade-offs
Build Social Value: considers users and broader impact
negative_signals: Analysis paralysis — can't act without perfect information
Works on comfortable tasks, avoids high-impact/high-risk work
Avoids conflict or honest feedback
Prioritizes personal credit over team outcomes
No evidence of thinking about user/societal impact

dimensions.name: Leadership Signals (E5+)
dimensions.weight: high for E5+, low for E3-E4
positive_signals: Drove technical direction for a team or project
Mentored others with specific, tailored approaches
Influenced decisions outside their direct authority
Built alignment across teams with competing priorities
Made unpopular but correct decisions and navigated the fallout
Elevated the team — hiring, culture, processes
negative_signals: Leadership examples are just 'I was the tech lead on X' with no specifics
No evidence of influencing beyond their own work
Mentoring = 'I answered their questions' with no investment
Cannot describe how they built consensus or handled disagreement
At E6: no org-level impact stories

level_calibration.E3: Clear STAR stories showing ownership and learning. Values alignment. Growth mindset.
level_calibration.E4: Deeper impact stories. Cross-team collaboration. Beginning of leadership signals.
level_calibration.E5: Leadership stories are required — mentoring, technical direction, influence. Org-level awareness.
level_calibration.E6: Must show org-level strategic impact, building teams, shaping technical culture.

dimensions.name: Requirements Gathering
positive_signals: Asks clarifying questions BEFORE designing — doesn't assume
Distinguishes functional vs non-functional requirements
Identifies key constraints: scale, latency, consistency, availability
Scopes the problem — explicitly states what's in/out of scope
Estimates scale: users, QPS, storage, bandwidth
negative_signals: Starts drawing boxes immediately without asking anything
Designs for the wrong scale (designs for 100 users when it's 1B)
Never asks about non-functional requirements
Tries to design everything — no scoping

dimensions.name: High-Level Design
positive_signals: Clear, logical architecture with well-defined components
Data flow is obvious from the diagram
Identifies the right core components (don't over/under-design)
API design is clean and RESTful/gRPC appropriate
Data model supports the requirements naturally
negative_signals: Architecture is a random collection of boxes with no clear flow
Missing critical components (no database? no cache? no queue?)
API design is an afterthought
Data model doesn't support the required queries

dimensions.name: Deep Dives & Trade-offs
positive_signals: Proactively discusses trade-offs: consistency vs availability, latency vs throughput
Goes deep on the most critical component — doesn't stay surface-level everywhere
Considers failure modes: what happens when X goes down?
Discusses caching strategy with invalidation approach
Addresses data partitioning / sharding with clear partition key reasoning
Considers evolution: how does this system change in 2 years?
negative_signals: Stays surface-level on everything — 'we'll use a database'
Cannot discuss trade-offs when asked
Says 'just use Kafka/Redis/etc.' without explaining why
No consideration of failure modes
Sharding strategy doesn't match access patterns

dimensions.name: Scalability & Production Readiness
positive_signals: Addresses horizontal scaling naturally
Discusses monitoring, alerting, and observability
Considers data consistency during scaling events
Mentions load balancing with appropriate strategy
Addresses deployment and rollout strategy
negative_signals: Design works for toy scale but breaks at production scale
No mention of monitoring or observability
Single points of failure not addressed
Vertical scaling only

dimensions.name: Communication & Driving
positive_signals: Drives the conversation — interviewer doesn't have to pull teeth
Structures the discussion: 'let me start with requirements, then high-level design, then deep dive on X'
Uses the whiteboard/diagram effectively — clear, organized
Responds to interviewer redirects gracefully
negative_signals: Waits for interviewer to ask every question
Unstructured rambling — jumps between topics randomly
Diagram is a mess — interviewer can't follow
Gets defensive when interviewer challenges a decision

level_calibration.E4: Solid high-level design. Basic trade-offs. Reasonable data model. May need prompts for deep dives.
level_calibration.E5: Drives the whole discussion. Deep on critical components. Production-ready thinking. Discusses monitoring.
level_calibration.E6: Staff-level depth: capacity planning, multi-region, system evolution, organizational implications.

dimensions.name: Problem Formulation
positive_signals: Translates business problem into ML task (classification, ranking, regression, etc.)
Defines clear objective function — what are we optimizing?
Identifies appropriate offline metrics (AUC, NDCG, precision@k) AND online metrics (CTR, revenue, engagement)
Considers whether ML is even the right approach
Discusses multi-objective trade-offs (engagement vs quality vs safety)
negative_signals: Jumps to model architecture without defining the problem
Can't articulate what the model is predicting or optimizing
Only discusses offline metrics, ignores online/business metrics
Over-complicates: uses ML when rules would work
Single objective with no consideration of trade-offs

dimensions.name: Data & Features
positive_signals: Identifies relevant data sources and their characteristics
Thoughtful feature engineering — not just listing features, but explaining WHY each helps
Addresses label generation: how do we get training labels?
Considers feature freshness: real-time vs batch features
Discusses data quality, bias, and sampling strategy
Mentions feature stores and feature sharing
negative_signals: Assumes perfect labeled data exists
Lists features without explaining relevance
Ignores data quality or bias issues
No consideration of feature computation cost or freshness
Doesn't address cold-start (new users/items with no features)

dimensions.name: Model Architecture
positive_signals: Starts simple (logistic regression / GBDT) and explains when to add complexity
Justifies architecture choices for the specific problem
Discusses model capacity vs data size trade-off
Considers multi-stage architecture where appropriate (retrieval → ranking)
Addresses embedding strategies for sparse features
Discusses training: batch size, learning rate, distributed training
negative_signals: Jumps to the most complex model (transformer!) without justification
Can't explain why they chose a specific architecture
No progression from simple to complex
Ignores practical constraints: latency, compute cost, training time

dimensions.name: Serving & Infrastructure
positive_signals: Discusses serving latency requirements and how to meet them
Addresses model size and inference optimization
Considers batch vs real-time prediction trade-offs
Mentions model versioning and rollback
Discusses A/B testing and experimentation framework
negative_signals: Ignores serving entirely — only discusses training
No consideration of latency or throughput
Can't discuss how to safely deploy a new model
No mention of A/B testing

dimensions.name: Monitoring & Iteration
positive_signals: Discusses model monitoring: data drift, prediction drift, metric degradation
Addresses feedback loops and how they affect model performance
Mentions retraining strategy and freshness
Considers fairness and bias monitoring
Discusses the full iteration cycle: hypothesis → experiment → analysis → ship
negative_signals: Train once, deploy forever mentality
No monitoring strategy
Ignores feedback loops entirely
No consideration of model fairness or safety

level_calibration.E3: Basic ML formulation. Reasonable features. Simple model choice. Understands train/eval split.
level_calibration.E4: End-to-end system. Good feature engineering. Model progression. Basic serving discussion.
level_calibration.E5: Deep on all dimensions. Multi-objective. Real-time features. Monitoring. Experimentation.
level_calibration.E6: Platform-level thinking. Multi-model systems. Efficiency at scale. ML strategy.

dimensions.name: Problem Decomposition for AI Delegation
positive_signals: Breaks problem into well-scoped sub-tasks BEFORE touching AI
Identifies which parts to delegate to AI vs code manually
Creates a clear plan/architecture before generating code
Delegates boilerplate/scaffolding to AI, owns core logic
negative_signals: Throws the entire problem at AI in one prompt
No decomposition — random prompting
Delegates the hard/interesting parts to AI and manually codes the trivial parts
No plan — just starts prompting

dimensions.name: Prompting Effectiveness
positive_signals: Writes clear, specific prompts with constraints and context
Iterates on prompts when output isn't right — adjusts rather than repeating
Provides examples or test cases in prompts when helpful
Specifies language, framework, and style preferences upfront
negative_signals: Vague prompts: 'write a function that does X' with no constraints
Repeats the exact same prompt expecting different results
Prompts are longer than the code they'd need to write manually
Over-prompts for trivial things (a for-loop, a variable assignment)

dimensions.name: Critical Evaluation of AI Output
positive_signals: Reads and understands every line of AI-generated code before using it
Catches bugs, edge cases, or security issues in AI output
Modifies AI output rather than accepting it wholesale
Questions AI's design choices and improves them
Tests AI-generated code with edge cases
negative_signals: Copy-pastes AI output without reading it
Can't explain what the AI-generated code does when asked
Trusts AI output blindly — doesn't verify correctness
Accepts suboptimal AI code without improving it
Cannot debug when AI-generated code fails

dimensions.name: Architecture Ownership
positive_signals: Drives overall design — AI fills in implementation details
Makes deliberate technology and API choices
Maintains consistent architecture across AI-generated components
Refactors AI output to fit the overall design
negative_signals: AI drives the architecture — candidate follows wherever it goes
Inconsistent patterns across components (each AI generation uses different style)
No opinion on design decisions — accepts whatever AI suggests
Final code looks like a patchwork of disconnected AI generations

dimensions.name: Scope & Velocity
positive_signals: Accomplishes significantly more than would be possible without AI
Uses time savings from AI to add depth (tests, error handling, monitoring)
Efficient workflow — smooth switching between manual coding and AI
Delivers a working, integrated solution
negative_signals: Achieves less than a no-AI interview despite having AI tools
Spends more time fighting with AI than coding
Solution is half-finished or doesn't integrate
AI assistance adds chaos rather than velocity

level_calibration.E3: Uses AI to handle boilerplate, understands generated code, delivers working solution.
level_calibration.E4: Strategic AI use, catches AI errors, delivers polished solution with good architecture.
level_calibration.E5: Masterful AI workflow, deep critical evaluation, production-quality output, tackles ambitious scope.
level_calibration.E6: AI multiplier — uses AI to operate at a scope that demonstrates staff-level system thinking.
